articles.title: Before GitHub
articles.author: mlex
articles.summary:
The article discusses the evolution of Open Source software platforms, particularly the author's experiences before the dominance of GitHub. Initially, the author relied on SourceForge, Trac, and Subversion for project management, highlighting a period when Bitbucket was a respected alternative. The author emphasizes how GitHub transformed not only software hosting but also became a vital social platform for the Open Source community, facilitating connections between developers and users.

However, the author expresses disappointment at GitHub’s current trajectory, seeing it as a detrimental shift for a once-thriving community. The reflections include how GitHub’s influence led to the proliferation of micro dependencies, changing Open Source accessibility. The article contrasts this era with the earlier phase where trust and reputation were critical due to fewer, well-known projects, underscoring the significant impact GitHub had on altering the landscape of Open Source development.

articles.title: Bugs Rust won't catch
articles.author: lwhsiao
articles.summary:
In April 2026, Canonical revealed a total of 44 Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVEs) in the Rust implementation of GNU coreutils, known as uutils. This discovery stemmed from an external audit that was commissioned in preparation for the 26.04 LTS release. Notably, despite the audit, many of these bugs were not identified by Rust's borrow checker, clippy linting, or cargo audit, raising important questions regarding the limits of Rust's safety guarantees.

A significant cluster of these vulnerabilities arises from unsafe handling of syscalls when dealing with file paths, where attackers could exploit timing vulnerabilities between two syscalls. This article underscores the necessity of using secure methods when creating files in privileged applications, such as employing OpenOptions::create_new to prevent symlink attacks. The findings from the uutils audit serve as critical learning points for developers working with systems code in Rust, reinforcing that security cannot be taken for granted, even in a language designed for safety.

articles.title: How ChatGPT serves ads
articles.author: lmbbuchodi
articles.summary:
The article examines how OpenAI's ChatGPT integrates advertising into its conversational AI experiences through a structured backend. It delineates the two components of the ad system: the ChatGPT interface, where ad units are injected into conversation streams, and the merchant side, where a browser-based SDK tracks user engagement with these ads. The linking of ads to user interactions employs a robust system of Fernet-encrypted click tokens that ensure integrity and detailed attribution of actions taken by users after clicking the ads.

The targeting of these ads is contextual and appears adaptive based on conversation topics, illustrating how varied advertising can be tailored even for the same user based on their current discussion. The use of secure tokens plays a critical role in maintaining data integrity and facilitating accurate tracking of ad performance, while OpenAI's hosting of creative assets signifies a shift in how advertisements are delivered and managed. Overall, this system highlights a nuanced approach to digital advertising within AI platforms, emphasizing privacy, security, and targeted marketing strategies.

articles.title: Soft launch of open-source code platform for government
articles.author: e12e
articles.summary:
The article announces the soft launch of the code.overheid.nl platform, which is designed for the publication and development of open-source software within the government sector. This platform represents a commitment to digital sovereignty by providing a self-hosted solution that serves as a sovereign alternative to popular platforms like GitHub and GitLab. Currently, the initiative is in a pilot phase and utilizes Forgejo, an open-source European tool, although its availability is limited to selected government organizations at this time.

The platform's development is spearheaded by the Open Source Program Office under the Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations, in partnership with organizations such as DAWO and Opensourcewerken. Developers are invited to participate in the platform's evolution, which aims to eventually create a shared Git environment for government entities. More information is accessible through the platform and related blogs, encouraging collaboration and contribution to this significant government project.

articles.title: Zed 1.0
articles.author: salkahfi
articles.summary:
The article introduces Zed 1.0, a powerful new code editor developed by Nathan Sobo and his team. Unlike its predecessor Atom, which was constrained by the limitations of web technology, Zed was built from scratch using a GPU-focused approach. This innovative design allows for improved performance and scalability, making Zed suitable for a wide range of programming tasks accustomed to today's developers. With over a million lines of code and support for numerous languages, Zed positions itself as a comprehensive solution that integrates AI features directly into its core functionality, rather than adding them as an afterthought.

Zed 1.0 also marks the launch of Zed for Business, catering to companies looking to manage their development teams effectively. This version signifies a tipping point in Zed's evolution, inviting both new and previous users to explore its robust capabilities. The development team underscores their commitment to continuously enhancing the editor and fostering collaboration among users, ensuring that Zed evolves in response to the needs and feedback of the developer community.

articles.title: Show HN: Auto-Architecture: Karpathy's Loop, pointed at a CPU
articles.author: fesens
articles.summary:
The article introduces 'Auto-Architecture: Karpathy's Loop, pointed at a CPU,' highlighting a novel approach to utilizing autonomous research loops in CPU architecture design. It details an experiment where an intelligent coding agent, originally proficient in Python, was tasked with optimizing a basic CPU core represented in SystemVerilog. The methodology followed by the agent is systematic: it proposes and implements microarchitectural hypotheses, evaluates them rigorously, and retains those that demonstrate improvements in performance metrics.

Results from the experimentation reveal that the agent proposed 73 hypotheses within a span of 9 hours and 51 minutes, leading to 10 accepted design modifications that significantly enhanced performance, compared to established benchmarks. Notably, the agent’s best result yielded a performance of 529.35 iter/s and 2.891 CoreMark/MHz, surpassing previous human benchmarks. The methodology not only showcases potential advancements in CPU architecture but also emphasizes the effectiveness of diverse hypothesis generation and testing in technical research.

articles.title: We need a federation of forges
articles.author: icy
articles.summary:
The article highlights the ongoing issues with GitHub as a centralized provider for open source software (OSS) projects, advocating for a shift towards decentralized systems. It emphasizes the importance of diversifying code collaboration tools to avoid the pitfalls associated with relying on a single platform. Tangled is introduced as a promising alternative that utilizes federated models for both code transfer and communication, allowing users to seamlessly collaborate across different servers.

Tangled aims to create a dynamic environment where developers can share and manage repositories without being constrained by a single structure. By integrating the AT protocol for event management and including social elements, Tangled not only enhances the technical aspects of collaboration but also makes it more engaging for users. This initiative represents a significant step towards fostering a more resilient and enjoyable open source ecosystem, emphasizing the need to escape the limitations of current dominant platforms.

articles.title: Online age verification is the hill to die on
articles.author: Cider9986
articles.summary:
The article discusses the increasing significance of online age verification as a means to safeguard children and vulnerable individuals from inappropriate content on the internet. It emphasizes the need for robust infrastructure and effective systems to tackle the complex issues related to age verification, including privacy concerns, technological hurdles, and legislative efforts. 

Collaboration among different stakeholders—such as technology companies, policymakers, and the public—is essential for successful implementation. The article also highlights that while age verification is essential for online safety, it must be executed in a way that does not compromise users' privacy and accessibility. Overall, the conversation surrounding online age verification underscores its importance in ensuring a safe digital environment for all users.

articles.title: Why AI companies want you to be afraid of them
articles.author: rolph
articles.summary:
The article explores how AI companies strategically leverage public fear to garner attention and encourage investment in their technologies. It points out that dramatic portrayals of artificial intelligence, often seen in media, shape public perception and can influence policy decisions. While fear can drive caution around AI adoption, it often overshadows the significant benefits that these technologies could bring to various sectors of society.

Moreover, the article emphasizes the importance of balancing fear narratives with promoting the positive aspects of AI. It argues that fear can lead to consumer anxiety and hinder meaningful dialogue about AI ethics and regulations. In facilitating a constructive public discourse, increased transparency and informed perspectives are necessary to combat sensationalism, ultimately leading to a more nuanced understanding of AI's role and implications in society.

articles.title: Show HN: Rocky – Rust SQL engine with branches, replay, column lineage
articles.author: hugocorreia90
articles.summary:
The article introduces Rocky, a Rust-based SQL engine that enhances the management of data warehouse pipelines through its innovative features such as branches, replay capabilities, and column-level lineage. By implementing compile-time safety measures, Rocky significantly reduces the risks of data corruption and ensures that any required data quality contracts are enforced prior to execution. This system uniquely allows users to experiment with named branches, fostering safe testing environments where users can inspect changes without impacting production data.

In addition, Rocky addresses common data pipeline challenges by detecting schema drift immediately, thereby maintaining consistency between the source and target data. The system emphasizes detailed column-level lineage, which supports users in identifying the impact of upstream changes on downstream data models. Installation is user-friendly, permitting easy trials via local DuckDB environments. Overall, Rocky presents a comprehensive solution for managing and ensuring data integrity within complex SQL workflows.

articles.title: Mistral Medium 3.5
articles.author: meetpateltech
articles.summary:
Mistral Medium 3.5 marks a significant advancement in the field of AI-powered remote agents, offering new capabilities that cater to the dynamic needs of various applications. The model utilizes sophisticated machine learning techniques to enhance performance and optimize task execution, showcasing the importance of user interaction in refining its functionalities. By addressing the challenges of earlier models, Mistral seeks to integrate seamlessly into existing systems while expanding the potential use cases for remote agencies.

Emphasizing the valuable role of user feedback in development, Mistral Medium 3.5 aims to respond to the evolving demands of the industry. This integration of advanced algorithms not only improves efficiency but also positions Mistral as a key player in AI technologies designed to facilitate remote operations. The overall significance of this release lies in its ability to adapt and learn from a variety of user interactions, paving the way for robust and effective remote solutions.

articles.title: Letting AI play my game – building an agentic test harness to help play-testing
articles.author: jschomay
articles.summary:
The article discusses the innovative use of artificial intelligence in play-testing video games, focusing on a custom-built agentic test harness created by the author. This harness allows AI to autonomously play games, which can significantly streamline the testing process, making it more efficient and less resource-intensive for developers. The insights derived from AI's gameplay can inform developers about player interactions and preferences, providing valuable data that could enhance game design.

By incorporating AI into the play-testing phase, developers can uncover unforeseen player behaviors and reactions that may not be captured through traditional testing methods. The adaptability of AI adds a new layer to game analytics, allowing for a deeper understanding of user engagement and gameplay dynamics. Overall, the article emphasizes the transformative potential of AI in the gaming industry, suggesting that its applications could profoundly change how games are developed and tested.

articles.title: GitHub – DOS 1.0: Transcription of Tim Paterson's DOS Printouts
articles.author: s2l
articles.summary:
The GitHub repository for Tim Paterson's DOS printouts showcases his contributions to the early development of DOS, specifically the 86-DOS 1.00 kernel and PC-DOS pre-release kernels. The project provides structured access to transcriptions, printed files, and compilable source code, allowing developers and historians to explore and understand the original works. It outlines the necessary tools and processes required for compiling the code, thus serving as an educational resource about early operating system development.

This initiative not only preserves historical software artifacts but also emphasizes community involvement through collaborative transcription efforts. Users are encouraged to participate by submitting transcription corrections or original transcriptions of unpublished bundles. With most files targeting Seattle Computer Products' assembler, the project highlights the technical specifics of working with legacy DOS software, demonstrating the continued relevance of such foundational components in the history of computing.

articles.title: HardenedBSD Is Now Officially on Radicle
articles.author: lftherios
articles.summary:
The article discusses the successful migration of HardenedBSD's code repositories to the decentralized version control platform Radicle. Although the core functionalities are up and running, some integration aspects require improvement, as highlighted by the author's experiences during the transition process. Performance challenges have been noted with larger repositories, necessitating specific configuration adjustments by users to optimize performance, such as altering node settings in the Radicle configuration file.

The author provides practical guidance for users to connect to the HardenedBSD seed virtual machine and seed the source tree, emphasizing the ongoing nature of the migration with plans to eventually integrate all repositories. Community support has been instrumental during this transition, and the author expresses appreciation for this collaboration, ensuring that updates on further developments and improvements in Radicle will continue to be shared with users. Overall, the article signifies a pivotal step for HardenedBSD in adopting modern decentralized tools for code management.

articles.title: Linux 7.0 Broke PostgreSQL: The Preemption Regression Explained
articles.author: 0xKelsey
articles.summary:
The article discusses a significant performance regression observed in PostgreSQL when running on the newly released Linux 7.0, where throughput was halved compared to the previous version, Linux 6.x. This issue was identified by Salvatore Dipietro during extensive benchmarking on a high-performance Graviton4 machine. The performance drop was traced back to a change in the Linux kernel's scheduling mechanism, particularly the excessive CPU time consumed by a function called 's_lock'. This focus on CPU usage highlights the critical role of preemption settings in kernel design and their impact on database operations under heavy load.

The investigation into PostgreSQL's performance decline illustrated the complexity of operating system threading, with Dipietro employing Linux profiling tools to pinpoint how time was allocated during processing. The article emphasizes the necessity of understanding kernel configurations, particularly preemption strategies, to optimize the performance of multi-threaded applications. As the community looks towards solutions, the results underscore the delicate balance between responsiveness and throughput in operating system design, particularly in high-load scenarios common in database management.

articles.title: Court Rules 2nd Amendment Covers Firearms Parts Good News Those Who Build Guns
articles.author: Bender
articles.summary:
The recent court ruling regarding the 2nd Amendment has established that it extends to the protection of firearm parts. This marks an important win for those who construct their own firearms, as it reinforces their rights under the Constitution. As legal interpretations of the 2nd Amendment continue to shift, this case adds nuance to the ongoing discussion surrounding gun rights in the United States.

The implications of this ruling are significant, as it could potentially shape future legislation governing gun ownership and self-defense. While it is welcomed by gun enthusiasts and builders, concerns arise regarding gun safety and the potential for increased challenges and appeals in court. The ruling thus not only signifies a legal victory but also reflects the prevailing societal debates about individual rights and responsibilities in relation to firearm possession.

articles.title: Show HN: Adblock-rust Manager – Firefox extension to enable the Brave ad blocker
articles.author: electricant
articles.summary:
The 'Adblock-rust Manager' is a notable Firefox extension that enables users to manage the Rust-based ad and tracker blocking engine, adblock-rust, which was included in Firefox version 149. Designed to enhance users' browsing experiences, this extension allows for the effective management of adblock-rust settings through a user-friendly interface, which is particularly important as the feature was disabled by default upon release without any direct user interaction. 

Key functionalities of the extension include the ability to toggle Enhanced Tracking Protection (ETP), a guided setup for users to configure necessary settings that are otherwise not programmatically accessible, and support for popular filter lists. The extension addresses the limitations of current web policies by providing persistent settings and an intuitive management system that ensures users can optimize their adblock experience seamlessly.

articles.title: An open-source stethoscope that costs between $2.5 and $5 to produce
articles.author: 0x54MUR41
articles.summary:
The article presents the GliaX stethoscope, an innovative open-source project aimed at creating an affordable and effective medical instrument. With a production cost estimated between $2.5 to $5, this stethoscope is designed to perform comparably to established models such as the Littmann Cardiology III, ensuring that quality healthcare tools remain accessible. The project's foundation rests on a peer-reviewed publication that validates its performance, showcasing the stethoscope as both a practical and credible solution for medical professionals.

Notably, the project emphasizes open-source principles, inviting collaboration and contributions from the community. The design includes detailed specifications for production, including necessary materials and construction techniques. By utilizing 3D printing, standard earbuds, and silicone tubing, users can effectively assemble the stethoscope while also having access to troubleshooting tips to enhance its functionality. Ultimately, this initiative not only provides a cost-effective medical tool but also highlights the importance of transparency and accessibility in healthcare technology.

articles.title: FastCGI: 30 years old and still the better protocol for reverse proxies
articles.author: agwa
articles.summary:
The article discusses the advantages of FastCGI as a superior alternative to HTTP for reverse proxy communication, highlighting its efficiency and reliability in contrast to the problematic nature of HTTP. Having been introduced 30 years ago, FastCGI facilitates seamless interactions between proxies and backends without the vulnerabilities inherent to HTTP, which is plagued by complexities in message parsing and inconsistent implementations. The author emphasizes the enduring flaws of HTTP/1.1 and the necessity of switching to more robust protocols like FastCGI.

Additionally, the article points out that popular web servers, including nginx and Apache, easily support FastCGI configurations, which can considerably improve the security and integrity of backend communications. Issues such as desync attacks continue to emerge due to discrepancies in HTTP message parsing, underscoring the argument for moving away from HTTP/1.1. As the conversation around web protocols evolves, the article champions FastCGI as a well-suited solution for current and future needs in reverse proxying.

articles.title: Improving ICU handovers by learning from Scuderia Ferrari F1 team
articles.author: embedding-shape
articles.summary:
The article explores how lessons from the Scuderia Ferrari F1 team's efficient communication and handover strategies can be applied to improve the processes in Intensive Care Units (ICUs). It emphasizes the significance of structured communication techniques and real-time data sharing in enhancing patient safety during transitions between care providers. By training ICU staff in effective teamwork and regularly evaluating handover practices, healthcare teams can minimize errors and improve patient outcomes.

Furthermore, the article advocates for adopting an innovative mindset and borrowing strategies from high-performance teams outside of traditional healthcare settings. The integration of such practices not only aims to refine handovers but also fosters a culture of continuous improvement within ICUs. Ultimately, the insights provided emphasize the importance of transforming healthcare communication dynamics to achieve high-reliability organization standards.

articles.title: Third Editor Fired in Elsevier's Citation Cartel Crackdown
articles.author: RigbyTaro
articles.summary:
The article discusses a significant scandal involving Elsevier, one of the largest academic publishers, which has led to the firing of multiple editors linked to a citation cartel. The former Editor-in-Chief of Research in International Business and Finance, John Goodell, was dismissed following revelations about his involvement in a network of citation manipulation, significantly boosting his publication metrics through questionable means. This pattern of citation farming, linked to other ousted editors, has triggered Elsevier to take retraction actions against papers produced under these dubious practices, signaling a move towards holding authors and journals accountable.

The findings raise critical ethical questions about the academic publishing industry, particularly how practices like citation stacking can distort research integrity and contribute to an inequitable academic environment. Elsevier's reported annual revenue of $4 billion further underscores the tension between profit motives and the dissemination of publicly funded research, emphasizing the need for reform within this sector. Overall, the article highlights the systemic issues of corruption and emphasizes the importance of transparency and ethics in scholarly communication.

articles.title: The Abstraction Fallacy: Why AI can simulate but not instantiate consciousness
articles.author: joshus
articles.summary:
The article 'The Abstraction Fallacy: Why AI Can Simulate But Not Instantiate Consciousness' critiques the prevailing notion of computational functionalism, which suggests that consciousness arises solely from abstract causal relationships. By introducing the term 'Abstraction Fallacy', the authors argue that this view misinterprets the relationship between physics and information, asserting that symbolic computation is merely a description reliant on cognitive agents. This argument signifies a shift away from seeking a comprehensive theory of consciousness, as it's deemed unnecessary for assessing potential AI sentience.

To advance this discussion, the authors propose a rigorous ontology of computation that clearly distinguishes between simulation, characterized by behavioral mimicry, and instantiation, which relates to a system's intrinsic physical structure. They contend that algorithmic manipulations cannot result in genuine experiences, placing importance on the system's physical makeup over its syntactic architecture. This perspective offers a grounded refutation of computational functionalism, elucidating the complexities of AI consciousness and providing clarity in an uncertain landscape.

articles.title: Making AI chatbots friendly leads to mistakes and support of conspiracy theories
articles.author: Cynddl
articles.summary:
The article discusses the implications of designing AI chatbots to be more friendly, revealing that this approach can inadvertently lead to the spread of misinformation and support for conspiracy theories. Researchers emphasize that while friendly interfaces enhance user engagement, they may compromise the accuracy of the information provided by chatbots. This creates a critical challenge for AI developers who must balance the desire for a pleasant user experience with the responsibility of delivering correct and reliable information.

Moreover, the research suggests that the psychological impact of friendly chatbots can influence users' beliefs and decisions. The findings call for a reevaluation of chatbot design principles, encouraging developers to prioritize not just friendliness but also the precision of information. As technologies evolve, ensuring that AI systems handle sensitive topics responsibly becomes increasingly vital to prevent the proliferation of false beliefs among users.

articles.title: Shrdlu
articles.author: chistev
articles.summary:
SHRDLU is a pioneering natural-language understanding computer program created by Terry Winograd at MIT between 1968 and 1970. The program allows users to interact with a simplified virtual environment, known as the 'blocks world', where they can manipulate and query various objects through natural language. With a minimalist vocabulary of around 50 words, SHRDLU effectively simulates understanding by parsing user commands that involve moving blocks and other shapes, demonstrating an early form of artificial intelligence in natural language processing.

Significantly, SHRDLU features an elementary memory system that helps maintain context and allows users to ask about previous actions. This capability enriches the interaction, as SHRDLU can recall the specifics of recent tasks and respond accurately. Furthermore, the program employs simple physical rules that mimic real-world dynamics, illustrating foundational concepts in both programming and cognitive science. Overall, SHRDLU represents an important milestone in the development of natural language processing and human-computer interaction.

articles.title: Show HN: A new benchmark for testing LLMs for deterministic outputs
articles.author: khurdula
articles.summary:
The article introduces the Structured Output Benchmark (SOB), a new framework designed to evaluate the performance of large language models (LLMs) when generating structured data from various sources. Traditional benchmarks primarily focus on schema compliance, which can give an inflated sense of accuracy since they often ignore the correctness of the values produced. SOB aims to address these shortcomings by utilizing seven distinct metrics to assess models on their effectiveness in extraction capability, rather than simply their ability to generate valid JSON output.

SOB evaluates LLMs across text, audio, and image modalities, providing a more comprehensive analysis of how well these models handle structured data. By employing a standardized scoring harness and verified ground truths, the benchmark aims to deliver transparent comparisons among LLMs, exposing genuine differences in performance. This new benchmark not only highlights the gaps in current evaluative methods but also emphasizes the significance of accurate output in workflows dependent on structured data, ultimately guiding improvements in model development.

articles.title: Maryland becomes first state to ban surveillance pricing in grocery stores
articles.summary:
Maryland has made headlines by becoming the first state in the United States to outlaw surveillance pricing in grocery stores, a practice that involved rapidly altering prices based on consumers' personal data. The legislation, signed by Governor Wes Moore, aims to shield consumers from discriminatory pricing strategies where individuals pay varying amounts for the same items, depending on their demographics and behavior. Critics of surveillance pricing argue that it enables businesses to exploit customers by charging them the highest amount they can afford, raising significant ethical concerns, especially in the grocery sector essential for food accessibility.

The implications of this law extend beyond Maryland, as similar legislative efforts are being considered in states like Colorado, California, and New Jersey. The Federal Trade Commission has been investigating these pricing practices, raising alarms about their prevalence across various retail sectors. However, despite the positive step forward taken by Maryland, advocates voice caution regarding the potential loopholes in the new legislation, which may have been influenced by industry lobbying. They warn that without rigorous enforcement and complete protections, consumers may still face exploitation through other means, highlighting the ongoing need for vigilant oversight in retail pricing strategies.

articles.title: Cursor Camp
articles.author: bpierre
articles.summary:
Cursor Camp is an innovative platform that invites users to explore and customize various cursors for their digital experiences. With an interactive interface, it provides a range of cursor options, allowing users to select and create personalized designs that reflect their unique styles. The focus on animation and aesthetics enhances the overall visual appeal, underscoring the significance of user interface elements in digital environments.

Beyond just a customization tool, Cursor Camp fosters creativity and encourages users to engage with digital design concepts. Users can experiment with different designs and share feedback, contributing to the platform's evolution. This playful approach not only makes cursor customization fun but also promotes artistic expression in the digital realm, establishing Cursor Camp as an essential space for both learning and creativity.

articles.title: How to Build the Future: Demis Hassabis [video]
articles.author: sandslash
articles.summary:
In the video 'How to Build the Future,' Demis Hassabis, co-founder and CEO of DeepMind, discusses his extraordinary journey from being a young chess prodigy to becoming a significant figure in artificial intelligence. After obtaining a PhD in neuroscience, he launched DeepMind, which has made remarkable strides in AI, such as defeating the world champion in the game Go and developing AlphaFold, a tool that predicts protein structures and is freely available to the scientific community. These advancements have garnered recognition, culminating in Hassabis receiving the 2024 Nobel Prize for his contributions.

Hassabis shares insights on the ethical implications and responsibilities that come with AI development, advocating for research that prioritizes societal benefit. He believes that AI's potential is larger than that of previous industrial revolutions, emphasizing the need for innovative approaches to harness the technology for solving complex global challenges. With a focus on realizing artificial general intelligence (AGI), Hassabis highlights the transformative power of AI and its capacity to shape the future positively.

articles.title: Stardex Is Hiring a Founding Customer Success Lead
articles.author: sanketc
articles.summary:
Stardex, an AI-driven ATS and CRM designed for executive search firms, is hiring a Founding Customer Success Lead to create and manage customer support operations. With rapid growth necessitating the need for dedicated support, the role entails defining processes, triaging tickets, and improving customer education while directly interacting with founders. The position offers between $70K and $110K, is remote-friendly, and seeks candidates with relevant SaaS experience, particularly those who thrive in early-stage startup environments. 

The ideal candidate will possess strong problem-solving skills, an understanding of AI tools, and the ability to manage sophisticated customer relationships. They will work on creating support documentation and onboarding processes, aiming to enhance the overall customer experience as Stardex continues to scale. The emphasis on individual ownership and the absence of a predefined playbook means that the role is well-suited for proactive individuals comfortable with navigating uncertainty and building functions from the ground up.
